title: How To Make YouTube Shorts Hooks That Go Viral Every Time

Most people are asking questions that nobody actually cares about. Okay? Nobody thinks about these things and it does not keep people up at night. That's the problem. A question like, "Have you ever wondered how people become so successful? Nobody cares." Okay? I mean, people care, but nobody genuinely cares in a super interested way. Well, there's actually three things that are happening at once here, but so much more of an interesting hook. Number one, off the bat, there's a ton of name recognition. Obviously, with Mr. Beast, you recognize the name instantly, so there's just instant context. The numbers so large that turning it down seems just genuinely insane. But that's cognitive friction because your brain just can't process that and move on. And then the viewer almost definitely doesn't know the answer. Unless there's some sort of Mr. B super fan, they're not going to know why he turned down a billion dollars. So them not knowing is going to make them slightly uncomfortable. So your brain wants to close that gap, to close that loop. And the only way to stop that is to keep watching. Now, a lot of people mess this up because they ask questions that nobody cares about or that won't go viral. Asking questions that are way too open. Why do some videos go viral? I mean, that has a billion answers. The viewer automatically populates like three or four answers in their head and fills in the blanks themselves and then they scroll away. So, you want a question where even the viewer's best guess feels kind of incomplete. So, the life hack for this one, the upgrade to this type of hook that I've found is to add something sort of contrarian, a contrarian hint or foreshadowing right after the question. So, take for example, if I say, "Do you know why the most jacked guys at the gym are always the weakest?" It's okay. But here's the upgrade. What if I said something like, "Do you know why the most jacked guys at the gym are always the weakest?" And it has nothing to do with steroids. Hook number five, the negative hook. So, every hook that we've talked about up until this point has been pulling the viewer towards something, whether it's a a question, a a revelation, a resolution, a payoff, something that they want. But what if we pushed them away from something that they're afraid of or something that they don't want to happen? And what if that worked better? This, in essence, is called the negative hook. And there's actually real psychology behind this because it's actually been proven that losing something hurts roughly twice as much as gaining the exact same thing feels good. So, when we're talking about hooks, a title like here's how to grow your small channel will perform less good than something like YouTube doesn't hate small channels. And here's the example video. YouTube doesn't [music] hate small channels. It'll be not a punishment, I don't want to say, but there will be a real consequence for closing this video. So, the formula is that we're going to state a specific negative outcome and we're going to make it feel like it could happen sometime soon, not just something crazy in the future. Even something as simple as saying, "This could ruin your life if you don't do it correctly." It's playing off of a negative emotion instead of a positive one. And [music] that spikes people. That gets them interested. Think about what you see on the news all day. All the headlines. Are they going to talk about man rescues puppy? Probably not. They're going to talk about, you know, this woman got kidnapped and she's gone for weeks on end. That's what gets clicks. Well, largely it's really not like a bunch of positive emotions. Okay, I'm just being honest. And you're going to want to imply that the viewer might be affected or they might already be affected. But you're going to position the video as you are telling them whether they are or not. Your audience is going to get fatigued with it. They're going to stop believing it. It's sort of like the boy who cried wolf if you guys know that story. So, reserve negative hooks for when the warning is actually real. And they'll hit way harder because of the contrast. And finally, the sixth one, the anti-hook. And this is the one I said almost nobody is using. But really, almost nobody is using this correctly. So, it's sort of a cheat code. Everything I just taught you, pattern interruptions, call outs, questions, comparisons, negatives, all of it is built on the same core idea of creating a gap for the viewer. make the viewer feel like they're missing something, they're losing something, and then pull them forward into your video. But the anti-hook actually does none of that. It just starts. Zack D Films does this better than almost anybody. He's the clearest cut example. Everybody knows who he is, and there's no introduction and no context. Watch the start of this video, the first couple seconds here, the hook. >> Jumped out as his plane was hit by enemy fire. But as he parachuted to the ground, they kept shooting. >> Who is this man? Does anybody know? Can anybody tell me who this man is? Do they know who this man is or who is even shooting at him? Does anybody know? The answer is nobody knows. You have been thrown into the story without any context. But when we open midaction and it just says he jumped out of the plane and he's getting shot at, your brain immediately starts filling in the gaps. Well, is this some sort of war? Do I know the war? Is this World War II? Is this World War I? Who is this person? What's happening? Why does it matter? All of these valid questions is what goes through your viewer's subconscious. And that gap filling is attention. To understand this, you need to understand that if the viewer is spending time trying to fill in the gaps, trying to figure out what is this situation, what's going on, rather than thinking about if they want to swipe away from your video or not, you've already won. That's the most important step. If they're thinking about what is going on and they're confused or they just want context, they're trying to fill in the gaps themselves, that inherently means that they aren't thinking about if they should swipe away from your video or not. And that's why when these hooks are executed correctly, they have some of the highest retention rates, the highest swipe rates out of any of the hook types that I talked about today.
